title: Context: Judaism and Christianity
description: Answer passage-based and map-based questions about Christianity in the Roman Empire.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

**Note:** In this context, "paganism" refers to pre-Christian Roman religious beliefs.

>Since the dawn of Roman history paganism’s representatives had received political privileges and exemptions from economic obligations to the state, while in return religion gave a moral support to political institutions. The new career of the Christian church that began with Constantine caused a vast change in this aspect of Roman civilization. The alliance of paganism and the Empire was dissolved; in its place there developed a union of the Christian church and the state. Yet the ancient religious institutions were so intimately associated with the national tradition and custom that the transition from the old order [paganism] to the new [Christianity] was a gradual one.

From: William K. Boyd, _The Ecclesiastical Edicts of the Theodosian Code_ (New York: Columbia University Press, 1905), 11. 

**Which of the following best describes the result of the transition from paganism to Christianity?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Boyd doesn't argue that Christianity quickly and completely replaced paganism.

Which of the following best describes Boyd’s claim about the transition from paganism to Christianity? 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Christianity immediately and completely changed the way the Roman government operated
Christianity quickly and completely replaced paganism in the Roman Empire
Christianity slowly replaced paganism and took over and expanded on its previous roles
Christianity had little influence on the Roman state

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage and answer the question: 

>In 386 the emperor gave the Arians [a Christian sect the Church considered heretical] the right of assemblage and declared it violators would be...liable to the death penalty. When Ambrose  [The bishop of Milan - at this time the Western Roman capital] again refused to allow the Arians the use of church property [the emperor] issued a decree exiling him. Ambrose replied that the emperor was within the church, not over it, and that in matters of faith the layman has no jurisdiction over the priest. The sympathies of the people were again with him, and [the emperor] did not attempt to enforce the decree of exile. 

From: William K. Boyd, _The Ecclesiastical Edicts of the Theodosian Code_ (New York: Columbia University Press, 1905), 47-48.

**What does Ambrose’s reply that “the emperor is within the church, not over it” tell us about the importance Christianity had achieved in the Roman Empire at this time?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Emperor was both the head of the empire as well as the head of the Church
Christianity had become so integrated with the Empire that its authority outranked the emperor
The political structures of the Roman Empire had largely rejected the authority of the church
Political meetings were held exclusively inside church buildings

assessment_items.question_content:
Study the map below and answer the question that follows:

[[☃ image 2]]



**Which of the following changes best explains the increased spread of Christianity after c. 300 CE?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The spread of Christianity was **not** equal in both rural and urban areas.

Prior to ca. 300 CE, Christianity spread


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 to 800 CE. Spain, France, Italy, Greece, Asia Minor, North Africa, Germany and England were all Christianized by 800 CE.
options.caption: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 to 800 CE. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/)

choices.content: Emperor Constantine made it legal to practice Christianity with the Edict of Milan
Emperor Constantine made Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire
The fall of the Roman Empire led to the end of persecution of Christianity
Christian Germanic tribes migrated into the empire and forced Romans to convert

assessment_items.question_content:
Study this map and answer the question that follows.

[[☃ image 1]]

**For which of the following claims could this map be used as evidence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Christianity didn't spread less between 300 and 600 CE than it did during the previous 300 years.

Between 300 and 600 CE, Christianity spread 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 to 800 CE. Spain, France, Italy, Greece, Asia Minor, North Africa, Germany and England were all Christianized by 800 CE.
options.caption: A map of the spread of Christianity from 300 CE to 800 CE. Dark Blue shows spread up to 300 CE; Green shows spread between 300 CE and 600 CE; Yellow shows spread after 600 CE.  Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=3416659)

choices.content: Christianity's spread prior to the ninth century was aided by it status as the official religion of the Roman Empire
Christianity's popularity allowed it spread far beyond the boundaries of the Roman Empire by the ninth century
Christianity spread through Europe mostly after 600 CE because the fall of the Roman Empire ended persecution of Christians
Christianity remained a predominantly urban religion even after the fall of the Roman Empire led to reduced urbanization

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage and answer the question. 

>In 386 CE the emperor gave the Arians [a Christian sect the Church considered heretical] the right of assembly and declared that violators would be...liable to the death penalty. When Ambrose [the bishop of Milan - at this time the Western Roman capital] again refused to allow the Arians the use of church property, the emperor issued a decree exiling him. Ambrose replied that the emperor was within the church, not over it, and that in matters of faith the layman has no jurisdiction over the priest. The sympathies of the people were again with him, and the emperor did not attempt to enforce the decree of exile. 

From: William K. Boyd, _The Ecclesiastical Edicts of the Theodosian Code_, 1905.

**Which of the following claims about Christianity in the fourth century CE Roman Empire could the information in this passage provide evidence for?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Christian leaders took over some of the authority that had traditionally belonged to Roman emperors
Roman emperors had complete control over Christian leaders and could exile them for disagreement
Church leaders and Roman emperors agreed on what Christian beliefs and behaviors were considered orthodox
The common people were unwilling to support Church leaders who contradicted the emperor's orders
